text: This video illustrates a novel approach to laparoscopic surgery of the duodenum. This operation is a laparoscopic Whipple procedure.
The surgeon opens the lesser sac, exposing the insertion of the gastrocolic trunk into the area of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA).
The surgeon attempts to expose the anterior surface of the duodenum, focusing near the hepatic flexure and gallbladder, using laparoscopic techniques.
I'm having a hard time. And in this case, I recollected a previous video that I have published on YouTube.
The surgeon utilizes a trans mesenteric medial approach to access the duodenum, revealing two gastrointestinal stromal tumors (GISTs) visible through the mesentery.
So much like you would for a laparoscopic right hemicolectomy, I approached the duodenum in that same fashion.
The surgeon visualizes the duodenum and a GIST tumor through the mesentery, exposing the anterior surface of the pancreatic head and duodenum.
The surgeon separates the pancreatic head from the mesentery of the colon, while mobilizing the remainder of the duodenum, revealing clear anatomical boundaries between these structures.
The patient is positioned in the same fashion as they would be for a laparoscopic right hemicolectomy.
The surgeon performs a full Kocher maneuver, revealing the inferior vena cava located posterior to the pancreas and duodenum.
The inferior vena cava (IVC) is visibly pulsating, while the duodenum exhibits excellent mobility, indicating successful mobilization and exposure of these structures.
The excised specimen is placed in a bag, and the surgeon prepares to wedge it out. The duodenum is clearly visible, providing an optimal view for suturing it closed.

The surgeon uses a two-layer running suture technique to close the duodenotomy, with the bowel occluded distal to the closure. A gastroscope is inserted to perform an underwater test, checking for leaks at the suture site.
The surgeon observes the duodenum, noting its good distention and the absence of any leakage, indicating successful closure and integrity of the sutured area.
I have used the same approach in cases where the mesentery is more fatty. This again is a gastrointestinal stromal tumor of the duodenum, but this patient has much more mesenteric obesity.
I essentially use the same technique and landmarks as well as patient positioning.

The surgeon tentatively elevates the iliocolic pedicle and the right colic, or possibly the right branch of the middle colic artery, to create a transmesenteric window. A flap is raised to improve access to the surgical site.
The surgeon exposes the duodenum and the tumor, with the pancreatic head visible in the surgical field. The duodenum is fully mobilized, allowing clear visualization of these structures.
The surgeon performs a full thickness excision of the duodenal wall, preparing for a two-layer closure of the excised area.
The surgeon performs an underwater test on the duodenum, observing its peristalsis through a gastroscope. The test confirms no evidence of leakage.
In the Trendelenburg position, the surgeon observes the duodenum emerging clearly through the mesentery, with adhesions visible tacking up the cecum.
The surgeon carefully mobilizes the duodenum using a trans mesenteric medial approach, providing clear access to the anatomical structures involved in the pancreaticoduodenectomy.
The surgeon mobilizes the anterior surface of the duodenum and pancreatic head, achieving clear visualization of the pancreatic head and the remainder of the duodenum.

The surgeon mobilizes the lateral and posterior attachments of the duodenum, revealing the inferior vena cava. A window is created to the left side of the mesentery, providing a clear view of the IVC.
The surgeon opens the ligament of Treitz, creating a window on the left side of the mesentery, revealing the anatomical structures in this area.

The surgeon demonstrates good mobility of the duodenum, with the fourth part of the duodenum and proximal jejunum visible through the mesenteric window. Due to insufficient laxity, the surgeon returns these structures and performs additional mobilization.

The surgeon fully mobilizes the duodenum, revealing the root of the superior mesenteric artery off the aorta. The proximal duodenum is visible, with attachments of the ligament of trites and mesentery being addressed. After mobilizing the proximal jejunum and distal duodenum, the surgeon divides the bowel using a laparoscopic stapler.
The surgeon flips the extensively mobilized duodenum and adjacent bowel into the right upper quadrant, demonstrating ease and comfort with the current surgical approach.
So in this particular instance, I chose to approach the duodenum primarily this way in another lapwipple.

The surgeon reflects the duodenum medially, using cautery to expose its anterior surface. The attachments posteriorly and laterally are taken down, revealing the inferior vena cava and the left branch of the renal vein. The superior mesenteric artery's origin is visible, along with the pancreatic head and anterior duodenum. The trans mesenteric mobilization allows full visualization of the porta hepatis and gallbladder.
